title: Pulmonary Embolism
oneLiner: Acute occlusion of pulmonary arteries by thrombus (usually from DVT) causing hypoxia, right heart strain, and risk of sudden death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Obstructive shock from massive PE** — single finding: hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or drop >40 mmHg) + signs of right heart strain (distended neck veins, loud P2, RV heave) → urgent echo/lung scanning + thrombolysis team activation.
- **Cardiac arrest from PEA arrest** — single finding: pulseless electrical activity (PEA) on monitor with no obvious cause; if suspected PE, give thrombolysis *without* waiting for CTPA (if local protocol permits) — call senior code leader.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** acute dyspnoea (most common), pleuritic chest pain, cough ± haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling/pain (DVT), syncope/near-syncope (high-risk feature).
- **Key exam findings:** tachypnoea, tachycardia, signs of DVT (calf swelling, warmth, tenderness, Homan’s sign — low sensitivity), raised JVP, right ventricular heave, loud P2, hypotension (massive PE), cyanosis (massive PE).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Pneumonia** — pleuritic pain + fever + productive cough + focal chest signs; CXR shows consolidation.
- **Myocardial infarction** — central chest pain, ECG changes (ST elevation/depression), troponin rise; pain is typically not pleuritic.
- **Pericarditis** — pleuritic pain but positional (leaning forward), widespread ST elevation, PR depression; no DVT.
- **Tension pneumothorax** — acute dyspnoea + chest pain + absent breath sounds + hyperresonance + tracheal deviation + hypotension.
- **Chest wall pain** — reproducible tenderness, no dyspnoea, no hypoxia.
- **Splitter:** “Dyspnoea + unilateral leg swelling → PE until proven otherwise”; absence of leg swelling does not exclude PE.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG — most common finding: sinus tachycardia; look for S1Q3T3 (specific if present, but insensitive), right axis deviation, RBBB, T-wave inversion in V1-V4 (signs of right heart strain).
  - Measure oxygen saturations (SpO2) – aim >94%; if low, give O2.
  - Assess haemodynamic status (BP, HR, resp rate) – if hypotensive, treat as massive PE.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, coagulation screen (baseline before anticoagulation).
  - D-dimer — high sensitivity, low specificity; **use age-adjusted cut-off** (age in years × 0.1 mg/L for patients >50 years). Normal D-dimer effectively rules out PE in low/intermediate pre-test probability.
  - Troponin (high-sensitivity) — may be elevated if RV strain; prognostic but not diagnostic.
  - ABG — respiratory alkalosis (low pCO2), hypoxaemia with increased A-a gradient.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) — gold standard if high pre-test probability or positive D-dimer in intermediate risk. Contraindications: severe contrast allergy, renal impairment (eGFR <30 unless discuss with radiology).
  - **Second-line:** V/Q scan — use if contrast contraindicated (allergy, poor renal function) and CXR is normal. Not suitable for severe COPD/parenchymal lung disease.
  - Chest X-ray — usually normal or shows atelectasis, effusion, wedge-shaped infarction (Hampton’s hump). **Normal CXR does not exclude PE** — essential for V/Q interpretation.
- **Diagnostic scoring:** **Wells score** (two-level) — stratifies pre-test probability:
  - Clinical signs of DVT (3), PE #1 diagnosis (3), HR >100 (1.5), immobilisation/surgery in past 4 weeks (1.5), previous DVT/PE (1.5), haemoptysis (1), cancer active (1).
  - **PE unlikely** ≤4 points → do D-dimer; if negative, no PE. **PE likely** >4 points → proceed to CTPA.
- **Never-miss test:** **CTPA** in any patient with high pre-test probability (Wells >4) or intermediate probability with positive D-dimer — delaying this can miss a treatable cause of sudden death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess haemodynamics: if **massive PE** (hypotension, shock, cardiac arrest) → activate thrombolysis protocol (alteplase) unless contraindicated; if contraindicated, consider surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy (discuss with cardiothoracics/vascular).
  - **Submassive PE** (normotensive but RV strain on echo or elevated biomarkers) → start LMWH and monitor for deterioration; consider thrombolysis only if clinical deterioration (discuss with senior/ICU).
  - **Low-risk PE** (no shock, no RV strain) → start anticoagulation with DOAC or LMWH bridge.
  - Give O2 to target SpO2 ≥94%.
  - IV access (two large-bore if shock).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue anticoagulation: LMWH for at least 5 days, then switch to DOAC (apixaban or rivaroxaban) if stable and no contraindications.
  - Monitor for bleeding (check FBC, coagulation daily initially).
  - Assess for DVT (leg ultrasound if symptoms).
  - If massive PE received thrombolysis, monitor in HDU/ICU for 24h (bleeding risk, re-occlusion).
  - If submassive, repeat echo or troponin to assess RV function improvement.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **DOACs:** apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days, then 5 mg BD; rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 3 weeks, then 20 mg OD. For warfarin: LMWH overlap until INR 2–3 for 2 consecutive days.
  - Duration: at least 3 months for provoked PE (surgery, trauma, temporary risk factor). Unprovoked PE (no clear risk factor) → minimum 6 months, consider extended anticoagulation (to be decided by specialist).
  - Consider cancer-associated thrombosis: LMWH for 6 months (e.g. dalteparin 200 IU/kg OD for 1 month, then 150 IU/kg OD) — per NICE.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** caution – excessive fluids can worsen RV failure in massive PE. Use crystalloid (0.9% NaCl) only if hypotensive and not in cardiogenic shock; typical rate 250–500 mL bolus, reassess.
  - VTE prophylaxis: **not applicable** – anticoagulation is therapeutic.
  - Diet: normal; ensure adequate hydration (avoid dehydration which increases bleeding risk on anticoagulants).
  - Observations: NEWS2 1–2 hourly initially, then 4 hourly if stable; continuous SpO2 monitoring if on O2.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line anticoagulation:**
  - **LMWH** (if using):
    - Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily (or 1 mg/kg BD – check local policy for twice vs once daily dosing).
    - Tinzaparin 175 units/kg SC once daily.
  - **Then DOAC:**
    - Apixaban 10 mg PO BD for 7 days, then 5 mg PO BD ongoing.
    - Rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BD for 21 days, then 20 mg PO OD.
  - **Alternatively,** warfarin: titrate to INR 2–3 (overlap with LMWH for at least 5 days and until INR 2–3 for 2 consecutive days).
- **Thrombolysis (massive PE):**
  - Alteplase 10 mg IV bolus over 1–2 min, then 90 mg IV infusion over 2 hours (max total 150 mg if weight >65 kg; otherwise 1.5 mg/kg). Check local protocol.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Fondaparinux (if HIT history) 7.5 mg SC once daily (weight-adjusted: <50kg: 5 mg; >100kg: 10 mg).
  - Unfractionated heparin IV (if need rapid reversal or during thrombolysis) – 80 units/kg bolus, then 18 units/kg/hr; adjust per APPT ratio 1.5–2.5.
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Active bleeding, recent major surgery (<2 weeks), history of haemorrhagic stroke, severe uncontrolled hypertension.
  - DOACs: avoid in antiphospholipid syndrome (use warfarin); caution in severe renal impairment (eGFR <15–30 depending on drug – see BNF).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - LMWH: dose reduce for CrCl <30 (enoxaparin – see BNF; tinzaparin – avoid if CrCl <20).
  - Apixaban: avoid if CrCl <15; reduce dose if CrCl 15–29 or weight <60 kg (10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD? Actually, apixaban no dose adjustment for CrCl 15–29 per NICE? Check local policy. I'll say [check local policy]).
  - Rivaroxaban: avoid if CrCl <15; consider 15 mg OD if CrCl 15–29.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Thrombolysis: giving alteplase to a patient who has a major contraindication (e.g. recent intracranial surgery, active peptic ulcer) — leads to fatal haemorrhage. Always check bleeding risk with senior before giving.**
  - **LMWH: prescribing a subcutaneous dose without checking weight (underdose risks recurrent PE; overdose risks bleeding). Always calculate weight-based dose accurately and document indication.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Death from obstructive shock or arrhythmia.
  - Haemorrhage from anticoagulation or thrombolysis (especially intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal) – if severe, reverse with protamine (for LMWH) or prothrombin complex concentrate (for DOACs/warfarin) – contact haematology.
  - Paradoxical embolism (e.g. stroke) via PFO/ASD.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)** — persistent dyspnoea/exertional intolerance post-PE; needs echo and V/Q scan for diagnosis; treat with pulmonary endarterectomy or medical therapy.
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome if DVT related.
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality: massive PE ~30% despite treatment; submassive ~5%; low-risk <1%. Most deaths occur within the first few hours. With anticoagulation, recurrence risk <2% per year. Unprovoked PE has higher recurrence risk.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always assess haemodynamic status first** — don’t be distracted by D-dimer or chest pain; if hypotensive, treat as massive PE immediately.
- **Use age-adjusted D-dimer** — routine use reduces false positives in older patients.
- **Document a Wells score** in the notes to justify CTPA request and reduce unnecessary scans.
- **If high clinical suspicion and CTPA contraindicated, order V/Q scan and start LMWH while awaiting result** — don’t delay treatment.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give thrombolysis in submassive PE without discussion** — risk of bleeding outweighs benefit in stable patients.
- **Do not rely on a normal D-dimer to exclude PE if pre-test probability is high** — go straight to CTPA.
- **Do not forget to check for contraindications before starting anticoagulants** — especially malignancy, recent surgery, active peptic ulcer, bleeding history.
- **Do not continue LMWH without a plan for switch to oral anticoagulant** — clearly document transition (e.g. start apixaban after 5 days of LMWH).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening dyspnoea, chest pain, haemoptysis, syncope.
  - Signs of bleeding: black tarry stools (melena), bright red blood per rectum, unexplained bruising, nosebleeds that don’t stop, vomiting blood or coffee-grounds.
  - Sudden severe headache, visual changes, weakness of limbs — possible intracranial bleed.
- **Follow-up:** specialist thrombosis clinic or anticoagulation clinic within 2–4 weeks; repeat imaging if symptoms persist; check for CTEPH screening at 6 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or any single parameter at level 3 (e.g. SpO2 <91%, SBP <90, GCS <13) → call medical registrar.
- **Massive PE (hypotension, shock, arrest)** → call critical care outreach / ICU team immediately; activate cardiac arrest team if PEA arrest.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L, rising, or deteriorating peripheral perfusion** → escalate.
- **Major haemorrhage** (e.g. haematemesis, melena, intracranial bleed) → call haematology, stop anticoagulation, reverse if needed, contact ICU.
- **If thrombolysed:** monitor in HDU/ICU for at least 24 hours.

rawMarkdown:
## Pulmonary Embolism
**One-liner:** Acute occlusion of pulmonary arteries by thrombus (usually from DVT) causing hypoxia, right heart strain, and risk of sudden death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Obstructive shock from massive PE** — single finding: hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or drop >40 mmHg) + signs of right heart strain (distended neck veins, loud P2, RV heave) → urgent echo/lung scanning + thrombolysis team activation.
- **Cardiac arrest from PEA arrest** — single finding: pulseless electrical activity (PEA) on monitor with no obvious cause; if suspected PE, give thrombolysis *without* waiting for CTPA (if local protocol permits) — call senior code leader.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** acute dyspnoea (most common), pleuritic chest pain, cough ± haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling/pain (DVT), syncope/near-syncope (high-risk feature).
- **Key exam findings:** tachypnoea, tachycardia, signs of DVT (calf swelling, warmth, tenderness, Homan’s sign — low sensitivity), raised JVP, right ventricular heave, loud P2, hypotension (massive PE), cyanosis (massive PE).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Pneumonia** — pleuritic pain + fever + productive cough + focal chest signs; CXR shows consolidation.
- **Myocardial infarction** — central chest pain, ECG changes (ST elevation/depression), troponin rise; pain is typically not pleuritic.
- **Pericarditis** — pleuritic pain but positional (leaning forward), widespread ST elevation, PR depression; no DVT.
- **Tension pneumothorax** — acute dyspnoea + chest pain + absent breath sounds + hyperresonance + tracheal deviation + hypotension.
- **Chest wall pain** — reproducible tenderness, no dyspnoea, no hypoxia.
- **Splitter:** “Dyspnoea + unilateral leg swelling → PE until proven otherwise”; absence of leg swelling does not exclude PE.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG — most common finding: sinus tachycardia; look for S1Q3T3 (specific if present, but insensitive), right axis deviation, RBBB, T-wave inversion in V1-V4 (signs of right heart strain).
  - Measure oxygen saturations (SpO2) – aim >94%; if low, give O2.
  - Assess haemodynamic status (BP, HR, resp rate) – if hypotensive, treat as massive PE.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, coagulation screen (baseline before anticoagulation).
  - D-dimer — high sensitivity, low specificity; **use age-adjusted cut-off** (age in years × 0.1 mg/L for patients >50 years). Normal D-dimer effectively rules out PE in low/intermediate pre-test probability.
  - Troponin (high-sensitivity) — may be elevated if RV strain; prognostic but not diagnostic.
  - ABG — respiratory alkalosis (low pCO2), hypoxaemia with increased A-a gradient.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) — gold standard if high pre-test probability or positive D-dimer in intermediate risk. Contraindications: severe contrast allergy, renal impairment (eGFR <30 unless discuss with radiology).
  - **Second-line:** V/Q scan — use if contrast contraindicated (allergy, poor renal function) and CXR is normal. Not suitable for severe COPD/parenchymal lung disease.
  - Chest X-ray — usually normal or shows atelectasis, effusion, wedge-shaped infarction (Hampton’s hump). **Normal CXR does not exclude PE** — essential for V/Q interpretation.
- **Diagnostic scoring:** **Wells score** (two-level) — stratifies pre-test probability:
  - Clinical signs of DVT (3), PE #1 diagnosis (3), HR >100 (1.5), immobilisation/surgery in past 4 weeks (1.5), previous DVT/PE (1.5), haemoptysis (1), cancer active (1).
  - **PE unlikely** ≤4 points → do D-dimer; if negative, no PE. **PE likely** >4 points → proceed to CTPA.
- **Never-miss test:** **CTPA** in any patient with high pre-test probability (Wells >4) or intermediate probability with positive D-dimer — delaying this can miss a treatable cause of sudden death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess haemodynamics: if **massive PE** (hypotension, shock, cardiac arrest) → activate thrombolysis protocol (alteplase) unless contraindicated; if contraindicated, consider surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy (discuss with cardiothoracics/vascular).
  - **Submassive PE** (normotensive but RV strain on echo or elevated biomarkers) → start LMWH and monitor for deterioration; consider thrombolysis only if clinical deterioration (discuss with senior/ICU).
  - **Low-risk PE** (no shock, no RV strain) → start anticoagulation with DOAC or LMWH bridge.
  - Give O2 to target SpO2 ≥94%.
  - IV access (two large-bore if shock).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue anticoagulation: LMWH for at least 5 days, then switch to DOAC (apixaban or rivaroxaban) if stable and no contraindications.
  - Monitor for bleeding (check FBC, coagulation daily initially).
  - Assess for DVT (leg ultrasound if symptoms).
  - If massive PE received thrombolysis, monitor in HDU/ICU for 24h (bleeding risk, re-occlusion).
  - If submassive, repeat echo or troponin to assess RV function improvement.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **DOACs:** apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days, then 5 mg BD; rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 3 weeks, then 20 mg OD. For warfarin: LMWH overlap until INR 2–3 for 2 consecutive days.
  - Duration: at least 3 months for provoked PE (surgery, trauma, temporary risk factor). Unprovoked PE (no clear risk factor) → minimum 6 months, consider extended anticoagulation (to be decided by specialist).
  - Consider cancer-associated thrombosis: LMWH for 6 months (e.g. dalteparin 200 IU/kg OD for 1 month, then 150 IU/kg OD) — per NICE.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** caution – excessive fluids can worsen RV failure in massive PE. Use crystalloid (0.9% NaCl) only if hypotensive and not in cardiogenic shock; typical rate 250–500 mL bolus, reassess.
  - VTE prophylaxis: **not applicable** – anticoagulation is therapeutic.
  - Diet: normal; ensure adequate hydration (avoid dehydration which increases bleeding risk on anticoagulants).
  - Observations: NEWS2 1–2 hourly initially, then 4 hourly if stable; continuous SpO2 monitoring if on O2.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line anticoagulation:**
  - **LMWH** (if using):
    - Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily (or 1 mg/kg BD – check local policy for twice vs once daily dosing).
    - Tinzaparin 175 units/kg SC once daily.
  - **Then DOAC:**
    - Apixaban 10 mg PO BD for 7 days, then 5 mg PO BD ongoing.
    - Rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BD for 21 days, then 20 mg PO OD.
  - **Alternatively,** warfarin: titrate to INR 2–3 (overlap with LMWH for at least 5 days and until INR 2–3 for 2 consecutive days).
- **Thrombolysis (massive PE):**
  - Alteplase 10 mg IV bolus over 1–2 min, then 90 mg IV infusion over 2 hours (max total 150 mg if weight >65 kg; otherwise 1.5 mg/kg). Check local protocol.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Fondaparinux (if HIT history) 7.5 mg SC once daily (weight-adjusted: <50kg: 5 mg; >100kg: 10 mg).
  - Unfractionated heparin IV (if need rapid reversal or during thrombolysis) – 80 units/kg bolus, then 18 units/kg/hr; adjust per APPT ratio 1.5–2.5.
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Active bleeding, recent major surgery (<2 weeks), history of haemorrhagic stroke, severe uncontrolled hypertension.
  - DOACs: avoid in antiphospholipid syndrome (use warfarin); caution in severe renal impairment (eGFR <15–30 depending on drug – see BNF).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - LMWH: dose reduce for CrCl <30 (enoxaparin – see BNF; tinzaparin – avoid if CrCl <20).
  - Apixaban: avoid if CrCl <15; reduce dose if CrCl 15–29 or weight <60 kg (10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD? Actually, apixaban no dose adjustment for CrCl 15–29 per NICE? Check local policy. I'll say [check local policy]).
  - Rivaroxaban: avoid if CrCl <15; consider 15 mg OD if CrCl 15–29.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Thrombolysis: giving alteplase to a patient who has a major contraindication (e.g. recent intracranial surgery, active peptic ulcer) — leads to fatal haemorrhage. Always check bleeding risk with senior before giving.**
  - **LMWH: prescribing a subcutaneous dose without checking weight (underdose risks recurrent PE; overdose risks bleeding). Always calculate weight-based dose accurately and document indication.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Death from obstructive shock or arrhythmia.
  - Haemorrhage from anticoagulation or thrombolysis (especially intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal) – if severe, reverse with protamine (for LMWH) or prothrombin complex concentrate (for DOACs/warfarin) – contact haematology.
  - Paradoxical embolism (e.g. stroke) via PFO/ASD.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)** — persistent dyspnoea/exertional intolerance post-PE; needs echo and V/Q scan for diagnosis; treat with pulmonary endarterectomy or medical therapy.
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome if DVT related.

### Prognosis
- Mortality: massive PE ~30% despite treatment; submassive ~5%; low-risk <1%. Most deaths occur within the first few hours. With anticoagulation, recurrence risk <2% per year. Unprovoked PE has higher recurrence risk.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always assess haemodynamic status first** — don’t be distracted by D-dimer or chest pain; if hypotensive, treat as massive PE immediately.
- **Use age-adjusted D-dimer** — routine use reduces false positives in older patients.
- **Document a Wells score** in the notes to justify CTPA request and reduce unnecessary scans.
- **If high clinical suspicion and CTPA contraindicated, order V/Q scan and start LMWH while awaiting result** — don’t delay treatment.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give thrombolysis in submassive PE without discussion** — risk of bleeding outweighs benefit in stable patients.
- **Do not rely on a normal D-dimer to exclude PE if pre-test probability is high** — go straight to CTPA.
- **Do not forget to check for contraindications before starting anticoagulants** — especially malignancy, recent surgery, active peptic ulcer, bleeding history.
- **Do not continue LMWH without a plan for switch to oral anticoagulant** — clearly document transition (e.g. start apixaban after 5 days of LMWH).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening dyspnoea, chest pain, haemoptysis, syncope.
  - Signs of bleeding: black tarry stools (melena), bright red blood per rectum, unexplained bruising, nosebleeds that don’t stop, vomiting blood or coffee-grounds.
  - Sudden severe headache, visual changes, weakness of limbs — possible intracranial bleed.
- **Follow-up:** specialist thrombosis clinic or anticoagulation clinic within 2–4 weeks; repeat imaging if symptoms persist; check for CTEPH screening at 6 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or any single parameter at level 3 (e.g. SpO2 <91%, SBP <90, GCS <13) → call medical registrar.
- **Massive PE (hypotension, shock, arrest)** → call critical care outreach / ICU team immediately; activate cardiac arrest team if PEA arrest.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L, rising, or deteriorating peripheral perfusion** → escalate.
- **Major haemorrhage** (e.g. haematemesis, melena, intracranial bleed) → call haematology, stop anticoagulation, reverse if needed, contact ICU.
- **If thrombolysed:** monitor in HDU/ICU for at least 24 hours.
